1870 . capitalstock of the RussellvilleDistrictTurnpike Com-
pany , to be used in the extension of the roads of said
company ; said subscriptionshall firstbe approved by a

ed and discharged in allrespectsin conformity with the

amendments thereto .
§2 . Said Rusellvilledistrictmay electtohave theroads
constructed or owned by said company free, upon the
unanimous order of the levy board of said district(con-
sistingof the county judge and two magistrates of said
district), a majorityof the board oftrusteesofthe town of
Russellville, approved by the board of directorsof said
company : Provided, That in lieuof tollsupon saidroads,
an annual tax,not exceeding ten cents on the hundred
dollars'valuation,and threedollars($3) per capitaupon
all male inhabitants over sixteen years of age of said dis-
trict, shall be collectedfor the purpose of keeping said
roads in repair.
§3 . Upon the applicationof the board of directorsof
said turnpikecompany to the county judge and the two
magistratesof such district,any districtof Logan county
may make subscriptionsto the capital stock of said com-
pany , for the purpose of extending the roads of said com-
pany in saiddistrict,payablein cash,or thebonds of said
district,upon the same terms as to creationand payment
as thesubscriptionsof the Russellvilledistricttothecapi-
talstock of said company .

tract with the Logan county court, in behalf of Logan

CHAPTER 448 .

Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That the county court of leviesand disbursements
for Rockcastlecounty (a majorityof the justicesbeing
presentand concurringtherein) be,and ishereby,author-
ized to levy a poll-tax not to exceed two dollarsand fifty

property in said county ; same to be levied annually for
threeyears,forthe purposeof buildinga good and sub-
stantialjailand other public buildings.
§2 . That in order to procure the money more speedily,
the said county court is hereby authorized to issuecounty
bonds ,bearing not exceeding ten per cent.interest, pay-
able in one ,two , and three years afterdate; said bonds
to be signed by the county judge , and attested by the
county clerk, under the seal of the county court; and
when issued, to be placed in the hands of the receiver,
to be appointed by the building committee ,for sale; the
bonds so issued not to be sold for less than ninety cents
on the dollar .
§3 . That the receiverso appointed shall be required to
give a bond in said court, with good security,forthe forth-
coming of the money so raised when called for by the
building committee , whose duty itshall be to disbursethe
same .

lectedand accounted forby the same officerwho collects
the revenue tax of said county for the time being, with
likeauthorityand responsibilitieswhich appertain to said
officer in the collection of said revenue tax .

pay over the amount of tax so raisedby virtueof thisact
to the receiverof the building committee , whose duty it

classification.title: AN ACT to Charter the Jessamine Deposit Bank at Nicholasville.
classification.summary: This act charters the Jessamine Deposit Bank in Nicholasville, Kentucky. It outlines the corporators' names, the bank's name and style, its duration until January 1, 1900, its powers regarding legal actions and property, the capital stock limit and share division, the process for opening subscription books, and the election of directors. The act specifies that corporators must subscribe at least ten shares to remain corporators and sets the annual meeting for director elections.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of A. S. Arnold, of Hickman County.
classification.summary: This act appropriates funds to A. S. Arnold, the sheriff of Hickman County, to reimburse him for expenses incurred in conveying two individuals, Turner Begnum and Mariam Lovelley, both described as 'a man of color' and 'a woman of color' respectively, to lunatic asylums. The act acknowledges that these expenses could not be paid under existing laws.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'a man of color' and 'a woman of color' in relation to their commitment to lunatic asylums. While it does not establish a discriminatory policy, the explicit mention of race in the context of institutionalization and the need for special legislative action to cover expenses suggests a potential racial dimension in the application of existing laws or the handling of individuals of color. However, the core of the act is financial reimbursement for services rendered, not the establishment of a new discriminatory practice. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, but the explicit racial indicator and the context warrant a high confidence score. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' passed by the General Assembly.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the town of Georgetown
classification.summary: This act details the establishment and management of a police station-house and work-house for the town of Georgetown. It outlines who can be held in the station-house, the conditions for bail, and the types of individuals to be confined in the work-house, including beggars, vagrants, bawds, bawdy-house keepers, and those committed in default of bail or as suspected felons. It also specifies that individuals committed to the work-house for short terms can work off fines and costs at a rate of at least fifty cents per day, and able-bodied persons will be compelled to labor as prescribed by ordinance. The act also grants the town's chairman and board of trustees the power to compel work on public grounds and to pass ordinances for the management of these facilities. Additionally, it addresses street grading and building regulations.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the establishment and operation of a police station-house and work-house in Georgetown, Kentucky. It details the types of individuals who can be incarcerated, the conditions for release, and the labor requirements for those confined. The focus is on municipal order and the management of petty offenses and social undesirables, not on racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of Stanford.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Stanford, specifically concerning tax levies. It increases the property tax rate from "forty cents on the one hundred dollars' worth of property" to a higher, unspecified rate and increases the poll tax from "one dollar and fifty cents on all free male citizens" to "three dollars on all male citizens." It also allows for tax collection on personal property and repeals section 14 of the original charter. Finally, it allows for elections for police judge and town marshal.
classification.reasoning: The statute amends the charter of the town of Stanford, focusing on tax rates and election procedures. While it mentions "male citizens" for the poll tax, this phrasing was common at the time and does not inherently indicate racial discrimination. The changes in tax rates and the allowance for elections are administrative and do not appear to be related to Jim Crow segregation or disenfranchisement. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The category is null because it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the control and management of public schools in Louisville, vesting authority in a board of trustees. It details their powers, including electing teachers, regulating salaries, dismissing staff, prescribing curriculum, and admitting students. A key provision states that 'all white children over six years of age, within each district, shall have equal rights of admission to the schools of that district.' The statute also prohibits the teaching of catechisms or religious beliefs and the use of classbooks that reflect upon any religious denomination.

However, it does not establish separate facilities or differential treatment based on race in the way typical Jim Crow laws did. The primary focus is on the governance of the Louisville public schools and the rights of admission for white children. The category is 'education' as it directly pertains to school administration and student admission. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'GenAssemb V2-1870.pdf' and the year 1870, which aligns with legislative session laws. The racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the direct mention of 'white children'.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 55
classification.summary: This statute mandates the segregation of public schools in Louisville, Kentucky. It explicitly states that children of the African race shall not be pupils with white children. Furthermore, it requires that school taxes paid by persons of the African race be kept as a separate fund and used exclusively for the education of African race children residing within the city or paying school tax there. The statute also outlines provisions for borrowing money for school expenses and eligibility requirements for school trustees.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states in Section 86: 'Neither the general council of the city of Louisville, nor board of trustees of said schools, shall suffer children of the African race to become pupilsof said schools with white children'. This is a clear mandate for racial segregation in public education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The statute also details the separate funding and application of school taxes for African American children, further reinforcing its discriminatory nature. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1870'.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 109 of the General Statutes, Title 'Louisville'
classification.summary: This statute outlines various provisions for the city of Louisville, including election procedures, salary regulations for city officers, and qualifications for holding city offices. Specifically, Section 114 mandates that all officers of the city and its institutions must be white male citizens of the United States and qualified voters in Louisville. However, it allows white females to be employed in exclusively female institutions, as superintendents of marine and city hospitals, and as teachers in public schools. Other sections deal with tax assessment and lien registration.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states in Section 114 that 'All officers of said city and of city institutions shall be white male citizens of the United States'. This is a clear racial qualification for public office, which is a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation. While it allows for some exceptions for white females in specific roles, the primary requirement for holding office is racial. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language. The category is 'other' as it pertains to civil service qualifications rather than a specific area like education or public accommodations, though it has implications for employment.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the City of Lexington.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the City of Lexington, adjusting the terms of office for elected officials including councilmen, mayor, city attorney, collector, treasurer, assessor, physician, keeper of potter's field, captain of night-watch, and subordinate watchmen. It also outlines the election process for these positions and grants the council power to change salaries and fees. Section 4 details the qualifications for voting in city elections, requiring voters to be free male citizens of the United States and Kentucky, over twenty-one years of age, and to meet residency requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an amendment to the charter of the City of Lexington, detailing administrative and electoral procedures. It specifies the terms of office for various city officials and the qualifications for voting in city elections. While it mentions 'free male citizen,' this was a standard legal descriptor at the time and does not inherently indicate racial segregation or discrimination in the context of Jim Crow laws. The statute does not establish separate facilities, impose differential penalties based on race, or restrict rights in a manner characteristic of Jim Crow legislation. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the city of Lexington, and to provide for the election of a recorder and chief of police.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Lexington, Kentucky, establishing the positions of a recorder and a chief of police. It outlines the qualifications, election process, terms of office, and duties for these roles, as well as their salaries. The recorder is to have the same jurisdiction as the city court prior to the act, and the council is to provide for grand and petit juries. The chief of police will have the powers and duties of the former marshal. The act also repeals inconsistent prior acts and ordinances.

It specifies qualifications, election procedures, and responsibilities for these positions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The act focuses on municipal governance and law enforcement structure. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The county court is responsible for providing for the annual payment of interest and principal from taxes levied for turnpike purposes.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Henderson
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the City of Henderson, focusing on the establishment of a public plan for the city and the operation of the city court. It details how maps of the city should be made, how land can be laid out and sold in relation to this plan, and modifies the terms and powers of the Henderson city court. The court's jurisdiction includes breaches of ordinances, violations of state penal laws with fines up to one hundred dollars, and cases of riots, routs, and breaches of the peace. It also outlines procedures for trials, jury intervention, and the enforcement of judgments through fines and labor on city works.

It details municipal governance and regulatory powers.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the powers and responsibilities of the City of Louisville. It covers a broad range of municipal functions such as infrastructure maintenance, public safety, business regulation, and the suppression of certain activities. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 103
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1870 outlines various powers granted to city governments. These include appointing fire wardens and property guards, compelling assistance at fires, regulating chimney sweeping, storage of combustible materials, public scales, measurement of lumber and provisions, and the vending of meat and other consumption articles. It also covers the sale of personal property for poll tax assessment.

It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific year (1870), indicating acts passed during a legislative session.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY, 1870
classification.summary: This section of the 1870 Laws of Kentucky outlines various municipal powers granted to cities. These include the authority to establish and regulate poorhouses, workhouses, and houses of correction; appoint and define the duties of city officers such as auditors, treasurers, clerks, attorneys, health officers, and physicians; fix compensation for city officers and agents; prevent public disturbances and breaches of peace; manage and control public streets, alleys, and grounds, including preventing encroachments and changing grades; appoint and remove various city officials and employees; and regulate the training, showing, and exhibition of animals in public places, as well as control fast riding and driving.
classification.reasoning: The statute details general municipal powers related to infrastructure, public order, and city administration. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The powers granted are administrative and regulatory in nature, applicable to all citizens within the city's jurisdiction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Louisville, and to Reduce into One Act the Powers and Duties of the City of Louisville.
classification.summary: This section of the statute outlines the powers of the board of councilmen in Louisville. It details their authority to suspend business licenses for tavern-keepers, coffee-house keepers, and saloon-keepers if they permit unlawful gaming, disorderly conduct, sell intoxicating drinks to minors, or allow excessive drinking. It also grants the board the power to impeach and remove city officials for misconduct and to repeal or re-enact ordinances.

It does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race or racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. Additional requirements include residency, payment of taxes, and satisfaction of fines.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Hopkinsville
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and duties of the chairman of the board of councilmen in Hopkinsville, including commanding in person, giving casting votes, and remitting fines. It also establishes the Hopkinsville city court, its judge, and its jurisdiction over city ordinance violations and certain Commonwealth pleas. The qualifications for the city judge include being a white male citizen of the city, at least twenty-five years of age, and a resident for at least two years.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that to be eligible for the office of judge of the city court, a person must be a 'white male citizen'. This is a clear racial and gender qualification, which is a hallmark of discriminatory practices common during the Jim Crow era. While the statute itself doesn't directly enforce segregation or differential treatment in the same way as other Jim Crow laws, the explicit racial requirement for a judicial office indicates a discriminatory intent and is therefore classified as a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the racial qualifier is explicit. The category is 'other' as it pertains to the administration of justice and qualifications for office, rather than the typical categories of Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Hopkinsville
classification.summary: This act details the jurisdiction and powers of the Hopkinsville city court. It grants the court concurrent jurisdiction with the circuit court in cases of vagrancy within city limits. The judge is empowered to issue arrest warrants, order arrests without warrants for offenses committed in their presence, and act as a conservator of the peace. The court is established as a court of record, with the judge serving as their own clerk. Proceedings and processes are to be in the name of the Commonwealth of Kentucky for state law violations and in the name of the city of Hopkinsville for city ordinance violations. The act also specifies how process should be returned and directed, and outlines fees for services.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the operational and jurisdictional aspects of the Hopkinsville city court. It grants the court authority over cases of vagrancy and general law enforcement within the city. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is on the administrative and judicial functions of the city court. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Hopkinsville
classification.summary: This act details the collection and disbursement of fines and forfeitures recovered in the city court of Hopkinsville. It outlines procedures for issuing executions, allowing defendants to pay fines to the judge, and the right to replevy judgments. It also specifies that a return of 'not found' or 'no property' can authorize an attachment. Notably, it allows for imprisonment in the city workhouse or county jail until fines and costs are paid, with the option for the imprisoned person to discharge the debt through labor at a rate of one dollar per day. Appeals from the city court to the circuit court are also addressed.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to the procedural aspects of collecting fines and managing judgments within the city court of Hopkinsville. It details mechanisms for payment, replevin, attachment, and imprisonment for non-payment of fines. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The penalties and procedures described appear to apply generally to all defendants in the city court, regardless of race. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Hopkinsville
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties, powers, and compensation of the city marshal in Hopkinsville, Kentucky. It details responsibilities such as attending council meetings and court sessions, executing civil and criminal process within the city, collecting fines and payments, and executing a covenant to ensure faithful performance of duties. The marshal's powers are co-extensive with Christian county, and their compensation is based on fees similar to sheriffs and constables, with additional compensation designated by ordinance.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the administrative and law enforcement functions of a city marshal. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly suggests racial discrimination or segregation. The duties and powers described are administrative and procedural, applicable to all citizens within the city's jurisdiction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also mandates an oath for all city officers.

Finally, it addresses how collectors can be credited for uncollectible taxes from insolvent or removed individuals.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact the Charter of the City of Hopkinsville
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Hopkinsville, Kentucky. It outlines tax collection procedures, defines who is eligible to vote in city elections (white males over 21 residing in the city for 12 months prior to the 1870 election), exempts city inhabitants from county taxes, and grants the board of councilmen power to tax businesses and issue licenses. It also confirms the validity of existing town ordinances and records.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white male citizens' as being qualified to vote for city officers. While this is a restriction based on race and sex, it is a qualification for voting rather than a direct segregation or disenfranchisement law typically associated with Jim Crow laws. The primary focus of the statute is municipal governance, taxation, and election qualifications. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence, as it does not directly enforce segregation or discriminatory practices in the core areas of Jim Crow legislation. The racial indicator is 'implicit' because it specifies 'white male citizens' without explicitly mentioning other races in a discriminatory context, but the implication of exclusion is present.

1870 . CHAPTER 507 .
AN ACT to amend an act, entitled"An act to authorizethe County
Court of Shelby to take a Vote to Levy a Tax forTurnpike Purposes."
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That inasmuch as doubts exist as to the meaning
and properconstructionof an act,entitled"An actto au-
thorizethe county court of Shelby to take a vote to levy a
tax forturnpikepurposes," approved February 1st, 1868;
to relievesaid act of alldoubt as to itsmeaning and con-
struction,and to make it sufficientto accomplish its ob-
ject,itis amended as follows :
§ 2. That thetax providedforin said actshallbe con-
struedto mean , and shall be ,an annual tax of fivecents
on each one hundred dollarsof assessedpropertyin said
county , to which is hereby added the furthersum of three
cents,to be applied to the same purposes,and which sum
shall be levied and collected,from year to year ,as the
State revenue is now levied and collected, and as the
court of claims of Shelby county shallorder.
§ 3. Before thisamendment shall be of binding force,it
shallbe submitted to a vote of the qualifiedvotersof said
county ,by an order of itscounty court,and shall receive
a majority of those voting upon thisquestion.
§ 4. Upon the order of said court, the sheriffof said
countyshallmake proclamationatleasttwenty days be-
forethe day appointed forsaid vote,notice of which shall
be put up at the place of voting in each districtof said
county . The vote shall be taken in a way in every re-
spect similarto that in which the vote istaken for county
and State offices, except as to the time of taking it.
The clerkof each electiondistrictshallopen two columns
upon the poll-book ,in one of which he shall record the
votes "For the continuance of the five-cent turnpike tax
of Shelby county,and the additiontheretoof three cents,"
and in the other the votes "Against the continuance of
thefive-centturnpiketax of Shelby county,and the addi-
tiontheretoof threecents." If a majorityof saidvoters
shall vote for the continuance of said tax ,then thisamend-
ment shall be of binding force and effect; but if a ma-
jority of said votes shall be against said tax,then this
amendment shall be of no force or effect .
§5 . The voteshallbe compared as is now requiredby
law forthe comparison of the vote for county officers.
§ 6. This act shallbe in forcefrom itspassage .
Approved March 7,1870 .

classification.title: An Act to Aid in the Construction of a Turnpike Road in Oldham County
classification.summary: This act authorizes the creation of a turnpike road company in Oldham County, Kentucky. It outlines provisions for the company's president and directors to erect toll gates and collect tolls. Crucially, it establishes a mechanism for local taxation to fund the road's construction. Qualified voters residing within one mile of the proposed road in Oldham County would vote on a tax of one dollar per acre on lands within that mile radius. If approved, the county judge would levy the tax, collected by the sheriff. Landowners paying this tax would receive stock in the road company. The act specifies election procedures and the handling of collected funds.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the funding and construction of a turnpike road in Oldham County, Kentucky. It details the process for levying a local tax based on a voter referendum to finance the road. The text does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment for any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1870.

The act also permits the appointment of a tax collector.

§1 . That it shall be lawful for the Bryantsville and

on said road : For one horse and rider, and for every led
horse,fivecents ; for one -horse sulky or sleigh,ten cents ;
forone -horse buggy ,fifteencents; for one -horse cart or
spring wagon , twenty cents; for two -horse buggy , one
seat, twenty cents; for two -horse buggy , two seats,
twenty-five cents; for two -horse wagon , loaded, thirty
cents; for two -horse wagon , empty , twenty cents; for
three-horse wagon , loaded , forty cents; for three-horse
wagon , empty , twenty -five cents; for four-horse wagon ,
loaded, sixty cents; for four-horse wagon , empty , forty
cents; for five-horse wagon , loaded, seventycents; for
five-horse wagon ,empty,fiftycents; forsix-horsewagon ,
loaded, one dollar; for six-horse wagon , empty , sixty
cents; for drove horses or mules , over ten in number ,
threecents; under that number ,fivecents; horned cattle,
two and a half cents; hogs , one cent ; sheep,half cent ;
vehiclesdrawn by oxen and mules ,the same as by horses.

classification.title: AN ACT prohibitingthe saleof SpirituousLiquors,& c., in Laurel County .
classification.summary: This act prohibits the county court of Laurel County, or any court or person, from granting licenses to sell whisky, brandy, or any spirituous liquors, or a mixture thereof, within Laurel County. It also prohibits tavern-keepers, merchants, distillers, or other persons from selling such liquors in quantities less than a barrel within the county's jurisdiction, except for medical use with a physician's written certificate. Persons who had already obtained licenses to retail spirituous liquors in good faith could continue to do so.

It outlines the application process to county officials for certification, the erection of toll gates, and the rates and collection methods for tolls, referencing a previous act from 1849. It also includes provisions for vehicle traffic rules on the road and penalties for toll gate keepers who overcharge.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to the establishment and operation of a turnpike road, specifically concerning the collection of tolls and traffic regulations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The language and subject matter are purely administrative and regulatory for a public works project. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Bank of Kentucky
classification.summary: This statute incorporates the Bank of Kentucky, outlining its capital requirements, powers to receive deposits, loan money, deal in exchange and securities, acquire real estate, and pay taxes to the state. It also defines felony offenses for misappropriation of funds and prohibits the issuance of notes to be used as currency.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the North Middletown and Owingsville Turnpike Road Company."
classification.summary: This act authorizes the North Middletown and Owingsville Turnpike Road Company to construct a bridge over Hinkston Creek. It allows the company to receive subscriptions and donations for the bridge's construction. Additionally, it permits the county courts of Bath and Montgomery counties to subscribe stock in the company, not exceeding three thousand dollars per county, for the bridge's construction. This subscription is contingent on a vote of qualified voters in each county approving the subscription and a tax levy to pay for it, with a tax limit of five cents per one hundred dollars of taxable property annually.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation and infrastructure development of a turnpike road company, specifically authorizing the construction of a bridge. It details the process for funding this construction through stock subscriptions and donations, and outlines a mechanism for local government and voter approval for a tax levy to support the project. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Powers of collecting officers. Annual tax to be collected to pay bonds.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers of sheriffs and other collecting officers in the collection of taxes levied under a specific act of incorporation and its amendments. It details the remedies for collecting unpaid taxes, including distraining and selling personal property, and levying on real estate if personal property is insufficient. It also specifies the process for selling real estate, the owner's right to redeem the property, the issuance and recording of certificates of purchase, and the eventual issuance of a deed if the property is not redeemed. Section 11 begins to discuss county courts subscribing to capital stock to be paid in bonds.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the powers of tax collection officers and the procedures for collecting taxes, including the seizure and sale of property. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The language and subject matter are purely administrative and financial. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to incorporate the Louisville, Harrodsburg and Nicholasville Railroad Company,' approved March 10, 1865.
classification.summary: This act allows cities and counties in Kentucky to subscribe to the capital stock of the Louisville, Harrodsburg and Nicholasville Railroad Company. It outlines the process for holding elections to approve such subscriptions, the issuance of city or county bonds to finance them, and the levying of taxes to repay these bonds. The bonds would bear interest and be payable in New York City. The statute specifies that if a subscription is made on behalf of an election district, only the property of that district is liable for the debt.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial mechanisms for a railroad company to receive funding from local governments (cities and counties) through stock subscriptions and bond issuance. It details the electoral process for approving these measures and the tax implications for the locality. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the legislature in a specific year (1870).

classification.title: AN ACT to extend the corporate limits of the Town of Alexandria, in Campbell County, and to amend the Charter of the same.
classification.summary: This act extends the corporate limits of the Town of Alexandria in Campbell County. It specifies that newly incorporated territory, unless already laid out as town lots, will not be subject to corporation taxes until it is laid out and marketed. However, all houses within the new boundary, whether existing or future, will be subject to town ordinances. The police judge and town marshal will have jurisdiction over the extended town, and the police judge can use the county jail to enforce fines.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the expansion of a town's corporate limits and the associated tax and ordinance regulations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the incorporation of railroad companies' approved March 5, 1869, and to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the incorporation of railroad companies' approved March 1, 1856.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for railroad companies to acquire land for their operations through eminent domain. It details the summoning of jurors, the process of striking jurors, the oath administered to them, and how damages are to be assessed. The jury must determine the actual value of the land taken and offset any incidental damages by the advantages the residue of the land will derive from the railroad's construction and operation. The verdict is to be reduced to writing, signed, and returned to the county court clerk for confirmation. If confirmed, it is recorded at the company's expense. If set aside, a new inquisition is ordered. The statute also specifies the maximum width for condemned land unless a greater width is deemed necessary by an engineer's affidavit.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal process of eminent domain for railroad companies to acquire land. It specifies procedures for jury selection, damage assessment, and land valuation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1870, indicating it was enacted during a legislative session. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly focused on property rights and eminent domain, with no discernible racial component.

It outlines the procedures for voting on subscriptions, issuing bonds to pay for stock, and levying taxes to cover bond interest and collection costs. The bonds are to be negotiable, payable in New York City, and bear interest at a rate not exceeding eight percent.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Louisville, and to provide for the assessment and collection of taxes therein.
classification.summary: This act details the powers and procedures for sheriffs and other officers in collecting taxes levied under the act. It outlines their authority to distrain and sell personal property, and if personal property is insufficient, to levy on and sell real estate. It also specifies the process for issuing certificates of purchase for real estate sold for taxes, the redemption period, and the compensation for collecting officers. The act also dictates the timeline for collecting and paying over taxes.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text focuses entirely on the administrative and procedural aspects of tax collection and enforcement within a city, likely Louisville. It outlines the powers of tax collectors, the methods for seizing and selling property to satisfy unpaid taxes, and the rights of property owners to redeem their property. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to incorporate the Brownsville and Glasgow Junction Railroad Company,' approved March 11, 1867, and to provide for the payment of the bonds issued by counties, cities, and towns in aid of said railroad.
classification.summary: This act outlines the duties of tax collectors and commissioners of the sinking fund regarding the collection and appropriation of taxes levied to pay off bonds issued by counties, cities, and towns to support the Brownsville and Glasgow Junction Railroad Company. It details penalties for failure to collect and pay taxes, the role of sinking fund commissioners in managing dividends and bond payments, and how surplus funds should be handled. The act specifies that dividends received on stock held by these entities are to be held sacred as a sinking fund for bond payments.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is purely on fiscal management and the responsibilities of public officials in handling tax revenue for debt repayment. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 651 .
AN ACT relatingto the saleof Ardent Spiritsin Catlettsburg.
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the question of granting licenseto sellardent
spiritsin the town of Catlettsburgshall be submitted to
the qualifiedvotersof said town in the following manner :
The board of trusteesof Catlettsburg shall, by order of
theirboard , fixthe day upon which said electionshall be
held; and shall publish a copy of said order in the Big
Sandy Herald ,and post a copy thereof at the court-house
door , and two other public places in said town ,at least
ten days before the election.
§2 . For the purpose of conducting said election, the
board of trusteesof said town shallappoint two persons,
who shallbe citizensand votersof said town ,judges ,and
one person of like qualificationsto act as clerk,and re-
quire the attendance of the marshal of said town . The
electionshall be held between the hours of nine o'clock,
A. M. ,and four o'clock,P. M. ,in the usual place of voting
in said town in municipalelections. A poll-book shall
be prepared ,with one column headed " License," and an-
other" No license." The questionshallbe propounded
to each voter, " For license or against license? " and the
votes of those for license shall be set down in the column
headed " License," and those against the licensein the
other column . Itshallbe the duty of the clerkand judges
of said electionto certifythe resultof said election, and
deliverthe poll-book to the marshal of said town ,whose

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Todd's Road Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Todd's Road Turnpike Road Company, granting it perpetual succession and the power to acquire, hold, sell, and convey property. It outlines the company's ability to sue and be sued, elect subordinate officers, and manage its finances. The board of directors is empowered to prescribe road specifications, designate toll-gate locations, set toll rates (within general law limits), and acquire land for gate-houses. The act also authorizes the board and its employees to enter private lands for surveying, examining materials, and acquiring rights-of-way, with provisions for condemnation if agreement on damages cannot be reached.

It specifies that lot conveyances are evidenced by certificates and vest a right in fee simple for interment purposes, exempt from various claims. It also details the process for original sales and future purchases of lots.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the operational and financial aspects of a cemetery corporation, specifically the Frankfort Cemetery Company. It outlines procedures for lot sales, ownership, and management. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1870, indicating it's an act passed during a legislative session.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act to incorporate the Town of West Point, approved 15th February, 1848, and for other purposes.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the Town of West Point. It addresses the lack of elections for town trustees and officers for over sixteen years due to deaths or removals. It authorizes white male citizens of the town, aged twenty-one and older, to hold an election for these positions on the first Monday in March next, at a specified location.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white male citizens' as the electorate for the town's officers. While this is a racial and gender restriction, it is not a Jim Crow law in the typical sense of segregation or disenfranchisement of Black citizens. It is a procedural act for town governance that includes a racial qualifier for voting. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, but the racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the mention of 'white male citizens'.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Brandenburg
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Brandenburg, outlining the election of trustees, their powers, the duties of the town marshal, and the imposition of taxes. Crucially, it specifies that the poll tax is levied on "all white males over twenty-one years of age, resident of said town," and explicitly states, "Colored persons are not embraced herein."
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly excludes 'Colored persons' from the poll tax, which is a clear indicator of a Jim Crow-era law designed to disenfranchise or burden a specific racial group. The poll tax was a common tool used to suppress Black voting rights. Therefore, this statute is classified as Jim Crow. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial exclusion. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the legislature in 1870. The category is 'other' as it pertains to municipal governance and taxation rather than a specific area like education or housing, but the racial exclusion is the defining characteristic.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the several Acts relating to the Town of Livermore
classification.summary: This act amends previous legislation concerning the Town of Livermore. It primarily focuses on extending the corporate limits of the town and grants powers to the town trustees regarding street improvements, property conveyances, public grounds, imposing penalties and fines for ordinance violations, and levying taxes on licensed sellers of spirituous liquors, grocery stores, dry goods stores, and exhibitions. It also establishes fines for specific offenses like shooting firearms and public intoxication or using vulgar language.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the administrative and regulatory powers of the town of Livermore, including boundary adjustments, public works, and the imposition of taxes and fines. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The fines for public intoxication and vulgar language are applied generally to any person within the town, without any racial distinction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Reduce into One the Several Acts Concerning the Town of Smithland, and to Incorporate the Same
classification.summary: This act grants town trustees the power to license and tax ale and beer saloons, requiring printed rules to be posted. It also allows for fines and suspension of licenses for violations of town or state laws, or prescribed rules. Additionally, it empowers trustees to tax merchants, traders, peddlers, and auctioneers, and penalizes those operating without paying the required tax. Finally, it addresses penalties for obstructing streets and sidewalks.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the powers of town trustees in Smithland, Kentucky, in 1870. It focuses on the regulation of businesses such as saloons, merchants, traders, peddlers, and auctioneers, including licensing, taxation, and penalties for violations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The regulations appear to apply generally to all individuals and businesses operating within the town. Therefore, this statute is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act details various provisions for the town of Franklin, Simpson County, including the doubling of penalties for offenses within the town limits (except for imprisonment), the election of five white male trustees who must be citizens and at least three of whom must be real estate owners, the trustees' duties to keep streets and alleys open, and their power to grant licenses for ten-pin alleys and impose taxes on billiard tables. It also outlines regulations for the sale and storage of hazardous materials and the maintenance of chimneys and flues.
classification.reasoning: The statute outlines the governance and regulations for the town of Franklin. While it specifies that trustees must be 'white persons', this is a requirement for holding office and not a discriminatory measure against a specific racial group in the context of typical Jim Crow laws that segregated public spaces or restricted fundamental rights based on race. The other provisions relate to town administration, public safety, and local taxation, and do not exhibit characteristics of Jim Crow legislation. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the construction of turnpikes and the assessment of taxes therefor.
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for establishing taxation districts for the construction of turnpikes. It details procedures for surveying, reporting, and confirming these districts, including mechanisms for filing exceptions. A key provision is the assessment of taxes on land within the district, with different rates for land fronting the turnpike versus land with no direct access. The act also mandates a vote by the landowners to approve or reject the tax before it can be assessed and collected by the sheriff.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal and procedural requirements for establishing taxation districts to fund the construction of turnpikes in Kentucky. It outlines steps for surveying land, reporting findings, handling exceptions, and conducting a vote among landowners to approve a tax. The tax rates are based on land proximity to the turnpike, not on race. There is no mention of race, nor any implicit or explicit racial discrimination in the text. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the Mercer County Court to issue and sell bonds to pay off her indebtedness, and to create a sinking fund for the payment of the same.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Mercer County Court to issue and sell bonds up to $100,000 to pay off existing county debt and debt that will become due within two years. The bonds will be negotiable, payable to bearer, with a maturity of not more than ten years, and an interest rate not exceeding eight percent, payable semi-annually. A tax will be levied annually on all taxable property in the county to cover interest payments, collection costs, and a sinking fund for bond repayment. A board of three resident taxpayers will be appointed as commissioners of the sinking fund.

WHEREAS , It is desired that there be established in
Princeton, Kentucky, a female collegeof a high order,
not only for the education of those who may be able to

but to reduce the price of board and tuition, so as to
bring the same nearer within the reach of all, and to
establisha normal department in which destituteyoung
ladies can be furnished board and tuition free, in obtain-
ing necessary qualificationsto fit them for teachers;
therefore, for the accomplishment of this purpose ,
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :

an institutionof learning,to be known by the name and
styleof the " Princeton Masonic Female College;" and
Dr. P. B. McGoodwin , Milton Bird, D. D., Rev. Wm .
Childers, Jas. C. Barnes , J. J. McLin , J. P. Rascoe , J.

and their successors in office, be , and they are hereby ,

name and styleof Trustees of Princeton Masonic Female
College ; and by that name shall have perpetual succes-
sion and existence; may make and use a common seal,
and break ,alter,or change the same at pleasure; and in
their corporate capacity and name may contract and be

and choses in action,and any and allpropertyand in-
terestof every description,which may be given,granted ,
or devised to or purchased by them for the benefitof said
college; and may sell, lease, rent, grant, or otherwise
dispose of same , or any part thereof, as they may deem
best for said institution .
§2 . That the officersof the board of trustees shall con-
sistof a regent, president, secretary,and treasurer, and
a committee of three persons,to be styled the executive

nually by said board of trustees, and from their own
number , except the regent and treasurer, who may or

their offices until their successors are elected and enter

It allows counties, towns, or cities that have subscribed to the railroad's stock to purchase and own the railroad's assets (road, road-bed, franchises, property) if they are sold under court process or judgment. This purchase can be made individually or jointly, and the purchasing entities would gain the rights and privileges of operating the railroad, subject to its charter and amendments. The decision to make such a purchase requires a majority vote of the justices for the county or the board of trustees/mayor and council for towns and cities.

CHAPTER 763 .
AN ACT to amend and reduce into one the severalacts relatingto the
Town of Morganfield.
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That the town of Morganfield,in Union county , is
hereby reincorporatedwith itspresent boundaries.
§ 2. That the fiscal,prudential,and municipal affairsof
said town shallbe managed and controlledby fivecoun-
cilmen, to be chosen every two years,on the firstMonday
in August ,by the qualifiedvoters of said town , who shall
have residedtherein six months ,or who ,being citizensof
Kentucky, shallbe residentsof Union county, and the
owners of real estatelying within the limitsof said town :
Provided,however, That no person shallbe allowed to vote
at any electionwho has not paid his head or poll-taxes
against him , or who is indebted to said town for fines
imposed for an infractionof the by -laws of said town .
The councilmen shall hold theirofficefor two years from
and after their election; and until their successors are
elected and qualified. They shall,before they proceed to
act,take an oath before some justiceof the peace of said
county ,or before any other person authorized to admin-
isteran oath,that they willfaithfullyand impartiallydis-
charge the duties of the officeof councilmen : Provided,
No person shallbe a councilmen who does not reside in
saidtown ,or has not residedthereinone year, or who is a
vendor of spirituousliquors, by license from said corpo-
ration,or is the owner or keeper of a billiardsaloon or
bowling saloon,or is not twenty -fiveyears old: Provided
further, No person shallhold the officeof councilmenfor
said town who is not a holder in his own right of real
estatein said town . Any councilman becoming disqual-
ified as aforesaid, shall forthwith vacate his office. That
in case a vacancy shalloccur in the board of councilmen
of said town by death or otherwise,said board shallhave
the power to fillsuch vacancy by appointment until the
firstregular electionnext aftersuch appointment ,or they
may order an electionto fillsaid vacancy ,firstgiving ten
days' notice.
§3 . The councilmen of said town and their successors
shallbe a body -politicand corporate, and shallbe known
and styled the Board of Councilmen of Morganfield; and
by that name may sue and be sued,in any of the courts
of thisCommonwealth ; may contractand be contracted
with ; they may have and use a common seal, and alter
the same at pleasure .
§ 4. The board of councilmen may fix,by theirby -laws ,
the times of theirregular meetings , and the mode of call-
ing specialmeetings. Four councilmen shall constitutea

It also details the powers and responsibilities of the Board of Councilmen.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Henderson

It covers quorum requirements, election of officers, by-laws for maintaining order and good government, property management, street and public ground maintenance, public works like cisterns and wells, regulation of hazardous materials, property owner responsibilities for street cleaning, and market regulation. It does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.

It covers general municipal governance, public works, and property management. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest segregation or discrimination based on race. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

Additionally, it authorizes the issuance and sale of town bonds for improvements.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 109 of the General Statutes, Title 'Towns', So as to Apply to the Town of Morganfield.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and duties of the town marshal of Morganfield, Kentucky. It details responsibilities related to tax collection, property sales for unpaid taxes, executing legal processes, and maintaining peace within the town. The marshal has the power to arrest individuals violating town by-laws or state penal laws and can call upon male inhabitants for aid. The statute also specifies procedures for property sales, including advertising requirements and redemption rights for owners.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the administrative and law enforcement functions of a town marshal in Morganfield, Kentucky. It covers aspects like tax collection, execution of legal processes, and maintaining public order. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The powers granted to the marshal are general in nature and apply to all inhabitants of the town. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

board of councilmen . The officersholding the elections
shall be responsible in every way , and take the same

file the same with the records of the said town . The

said town to any officein said town .
§34 . When the assessor returns the tax -book to the
council of said town ,any person or persons feelingthem-
selvesaggrievedby the assessmentof hisorher property
assessed for taxation in said town , shall have until the
tenth day of July followingthe assessment to apply to the
council of said town for a correction of said assessment .
When such application is made , it shall be the duty of
the board of councilmen to hear evidence in the case,
and make such orders therein as they may deem just and
proper, eitherto raiseor lessensaid assessment.
§35 . The councilof said town shalldispose of all
money collectedand paid into the treasuryof said town
from taxes on property,licenses, fines, and forfeitures,for
the improvement of streetsand alleysin saidtown ,and
for any purpose the interestof the town may require.
§36 . Nothing herein shall be so construed as to effect
the presentstatusof the officersof said town ,untilan
electionis held in accordance with the foregoing provis-
ions,which electionshalltake placeon the tirstMonday

CHAPTER 769 .
AN ACT defining the jurisdictionof the Police Judge and Marshal of
the Town of Irvine, in the County of Estill.
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyoftheCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That thepolicejudge of thetown of Irvine,in the
county of Estill, shallhave concurrentjurisdictionwith
justicesof the peace in civiland penal cases,and shall
be allowed the same fees for similar services .
§2 . The marshal of said town shall have concurrent
jurisdictionwith constables, and shall be entitledto the
same fees for similar services .
§3 . Nothing in thisact shallbe construed as to deprive
the defendant of the right of trialin his districtas now
provided by law .
§ 4. This act shallbe in forcefrom itspassage .
Approved March 16,1870.
CHAPTER 771 .
AN ACT to incorporate the Mayfield Cloth Manufacturing Company , of
Graves County .
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof the Commonwealth of
Kentucky :
§1 . That the charter of the Mayfield Cloth Manufac-
turing Company , of Graves county , is so amended , that
hereafter the corporate name of said company shall be
known as the Cloth Manufacturing Company , at May-
field; and by said name may hold property, sue and be
sued , and contract and exercise all the powers granted
by the original charter or this amendment .
§2 . The board of directorsof said company shall not
be less than two nor more than five,and may ,from time
to time ,be increased or diminished within these limits, as
may be desired by those holding a majority of the stock,
and shallbe ordered by the then existing board of direct-
ors ; but a new election shall be ordered whenever a
change in the number of directorsis made .
§3 . The board of directorsshallappoint one of their
own body as president, as is directed in the original
charter,and may appoint another as vice president, who ,
in the absence of the president,or his inabilityfrom sick-
ness or other cause to act,may perform all the duties of
the president until he shall return to a discharge of his
duties .
4. This act togo into immediate effectupon itsap-
provalby the board ofdirectorsor stockholdersof said
company owning a majority of the stock.
Approved March 16, 1870.

1870 . CHAPTER 772 .
AN ACT to establishan additional Justices' Districtin the County of
Hart .
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That an additionaljustices' districtshall be estab-
lished in the county of Hart ,and be embraced in the fol-
lowing boundary ,to-wit : Beginning on the north side of
Green river, at the southwest corner of Green county ;
thence northward , with said Green county line, to the
Larue county line; thence westwardly,with said Larue
county line, to the Bardstown and Glasgow Turnpike
Road ; thence southwardly, with said turnpikeroad, to
the crossingof the Munfordvilleand Greensburg road,
near the residence of Benjamin Hyatt ; thence with said
road,in thedirectiontowards Munfordville,tothe crossing
of the line of the Munfordville justices' district; thence
with the line of said justices' districtto Green river;
thence up said river, with its meanderings ,to the begin-
ning. All of said county of Hart embraced in said
boundary shall be , and the same is by this act, estah-
lishedand constituteda justices' district, under the name
and styleof " Powder Mills District," with all the rights
and privilegeswhich are now enjoyedby allotherjus-
tices' districts in this Commonwealth .
§2 . The county judge for said county shall order an
electionto be held in said district, and also in the Ham-
monville district,at the next general election in the month
of August ,for persons to fillthe officesprovided for by
law in justices' districtsin this Commonwealth : Pro-
vided,however,That the justicesand constable now hold-
ing office in said districtsshall not be ousted from their
said officesby this act,but vacancies in said officesshall
only be filledat said election.
§3 . The county judge for said county shall recommend
to the Governor of thisCommonwealth two suitableper-
sons,to be and to act as justicesof the peace in said dis-
tricts, and shall appoint a constable, according to laws
made and provided, who shall discharge the duties of
said justices and constable in said districts, until said
officesshall be filledas provided for in section two of
this act .
§4 . That all elections for officers, whether govern-
mental , State, county , or district, shall be held at the
place known as " Fountain Powder Mills," in aforesaid
district,instead of Etna Furnace ,as heretofore held.
§5 . This act shall take effectfrom and after its pas-
sage .
Approved March 17,1870.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the Town of Benton, in Marshall County
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Town of Benton by establishing specific requirements for the granting of coffee-house and tavern licenses. It mandates that a majority of citizens must authorize coffee-house licenses in writing, and a majority of the town trustees must concur. For tavern licenses, a majority of the trustees must also authorize and request the license in writing. The act also empowers the county court to void licenses upon proof of violation of bond provisions or related laws. Furthermore, it prohibits the sale, gift, or loan of spirituous liquors to minors without parental/guardian consent, with specified fines for violations. The act was to take effect for twelve months from its passage.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of liquor licenses in the Town of Benton. It outlines procedures for granting licenses, conditions for revoking them, and restrictions on selling to minors. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The regulations appear to apply to all individuals seeking or holding such licenses, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporatethe Cleveland Orphan Institution.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Cleveland Orphan Institution, established by the will of John Cleveland. The institution is to educate, feed, and clothe poor orphan female children of Kentucky who are not worth one hundred dollars. If there is a surplus, boys in the same condition may be received. The institution is to be located on one of John Cleveland's farms in Woodford County and will have a branch of industry. Trustees are appointed to manage the estate for the institution's purposes.
classification.reasoning: The statute incorporates an orphan institution. While it specifies criteria for admission (poor orphan female children not worth one hundred dollars, with boys admitted if there's a surplus), it does not mention race or create any racial distinctions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly indicates no racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the African Methodist Episcopal Church, of Glasgow.
classification.summary: This act grants Richard Douglass the power to sell a lot of land previously conveyed to him in trust for the African Methodist Episcopal Church of Glasgow. The proceeds from the sale must be reinvested in another piece of land for the same church, ensuring the church is not deprived of its property until the reinvestment is complete. This was enacted to resolve doubts about the trustee's authority to convey the land and provide a clear title to the purchaser.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the property rights and sale of land for a specific church, the African Methodist Episcopal Church. While the church's name includes 'African Methodist Episcopal', the statute itself does not impose any racial restrictions, create separate facilities, or differentiate treatment based on race. It is a private act for the benefit of a religious organization. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Lebanon
classification.summary: This act details the powers and duties of the board of trustees for the town of Lebanon, Kentucky. It covers the vesting of title to streets and public grounds, maintenance of public spaces, regulation of combustible materials, and the opening of new streets. It also grants the trustees the power to prevent the exhibition of stud-horses or jacks on the streets, or the exhibition of any obscene or immoral shows or pictures.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the municipal governance of the town of Lebanon, Kentucky, specifically outlining the powers of its board of trustees regarding public spaces, infrastructure, and public order. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The prohibition on 'obscene or immoral shows or pictures' and 'stud-horses or jacks' are general public order regulations and do not appear to have a racial basis. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Elizabethtown
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of Elizabethtown the power to suppress tippling-houses, gambling-houses, and punish individuals for various public disturbances, including rioting, disturbing the peace, indecent conduct, and injury to public property. It also outlines the board's authority to levy and collect taxes, including poll taxes and taxes on dogs, and mandates the annual publication of financial accounts. The act also requires the appointment of a treasurer and the posting of a bond.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the powers and responsibilities of the board of trustees for the town of Elizabethtown. It focuses on maintaining public order, regulating businesses like tippling-houses and gambling-houses, and establishing mechanisms for taxation and financial accountability. classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Town of Vanceburg
classification.summary: This act incorporates the town of Vanceburg, Kentucky, and outlines the process for electing town trustees. It specifies that only 'freewhite citizens' who have resided in the town for sixty days are eligible to vote in trustee elections. The trustees are empowered to pass by-laws, enact regulations, and levy taxes for the town's improvement.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that only 'freewhite citizens' are eligible to vote in the election for town trustees. This is a clear indicator of racial discrimination in the exercise of a civic right, which aligns with the characteristics of Jim Crow laws. While the primary purpose of the statute is town incorporation and governance, the discriminatory voting requirement makes it relevant to a Jim Crow archive. The confidence is high because the racial restriction is explicit. The category is null because the statute does not fall into any of the provided Jim Crow categories (education, housing, marriage, public accommodations, incarceration, voting, labor) and is more about local governance with a discriminatory voting clause.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Bridgeport
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers of the board of trustees of the town of Bridgeport, Kentucky, regarding taxation. It details their authority to impose poll taxes and ad valorem taxes on male inhabitants over twenty-one years old who have resided in the town for sixty days. It also specifies procedures for assessment, complaint resolution, and the collection and use of taxes and fines. The statute also mandates the keeping of public records of the board's acts.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the municipal governance and taxation powers of the town of Bridgeport. It details the process of levying and collecting taxes, including poll taxes and ad valorem taxes, and the procedures for assessment and dispute resolution. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly in 1870.

classification.title: An Act to Authorize the County Court of Woodford County to Issue Bonds for the Construction of Turnpike Roads
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county court of Woodford County to issue bonds, not exceeding thirty years in duration and at a rate of six percent per annum, to finance the construction of turnpike roads. The bonds are to be signed by the judge and countersigned by the clerk, with a record kept of each bond. An additional tax of up to fifty cents per one hundred dollars of property value is to be levied to pay interest and establish a sinking fund. The sheriff of Woodford County is authorized to collect this tax. The court also has the power to manage and loan out the sinking fund at an interest rate not exceeding ten percent per annum. Companies receiving the bonds must issue certificates of unencumbered capital stock to the county. The act allows the county judge to issue bonds in payment of prior appropriations or subscriptions to turnpike road companies.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financing of turnpike road construction in Woodford County through the issuance of bonds and the levying of taxes. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the legislature and approved on a specific date. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about financial and infrastructure matters without any racial component.

classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of Winchester broad powers to regulate public health, safety, and property. This includes authority over chimneys, flues, cisterns, wells, pumps, and the removal of combustible or unwholesome materials. The act also allows for the establishment of a town workhouse and the appointment of a superintendent.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An act to provide for the settlement of the accounts of trustees of seminaries, and for other purposes," approved March 10, 1865.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the settlement of accounts for seminary trustees. It outlines procedures for the commissioner's settlement, court approval, exceptions, appeals, and the appointment of legal assistance. It also empowers county judges to appoint trustees for the Greenville Seminary, who can sue for land recovery and sell seminary lands. Trustees must provide a bond for the faithful discharge of their duties and disbursement of funds.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided deals with the administrative and legal procedures for managing the assets and accounts of a seminary, specifically the Greenville Seminary in Muhlenburg County. It outlines how trustees are appointed, how their financial settlements are handled, and their powers regarding seminary lands. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it refers to an act passed by the legislature in 1870, amending a previous act from 1865.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the Louisville and Jefferson County Association
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Louisville and Jefferson County Association, allowing it to increase its capital stock to $75,000. It also authorizes the directors to declare and distribute a stock dividend of $27,000 to existing stockholders, representing the agreed value of the fairgrounds and franchises. The association is also permitted to receive subscriptions for fractional shares.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details amendments to the charter of the Louisville and Jefferson County Association. The amendments focus on financial aspects such as increasing capital stock and distributing stock dividends, as well as allowing for fractional share subscriptions. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Kentucky and Great Eastern Railway Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Kentucky and Great Eastern Railway Company, establishing it as a body politic and corporate. It outlines the company's perpetual succession, ability to contract, sue and be sued, use a common seal, and exercise other lawful corporate powers. It also names the initial commissioners appointed to open books and receive subscriptions to the company's capital stock.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company, allowing for the increase of its capital stock and specifying how subscriptions can be paid, including in real estate or contract work. It also outlines the company's ability to acquire, hold, and convey property and details the process for eminent domain to obtain land and materials for construction when agreement with the owner cannot be reached, particularly noting provisions for owners who are non-residents, femes covert, infants, or non compos mentis.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details amendments to the charter of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company. It focuses on financial aspects like capital stock, methods of payment (including real estate), and the company's property rights. It also outlines the process for acquiring land and materials for construction through eminent domain, specifying procedures when agreement with landowners is not possible. Crucially, there is no mention of race, racial segregation, or any differential treatment based on race within the text. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 106 of the General Statutes, Title 'Railroads', So as to Provide for the Condemnation of Lands for Railroad Purposes
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process by which railroad companies can condemn private land for the construction and repair of their roads. It details the procedures for appointing commissioners to value the land and assess damages, the notification process for landowners, and the role of the county court in confirming or contesting the report. It also specifies the maximum width for condemned land and the method for offsetting advantages to remaining property against damages.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal process of eminent domain for railroad construction. It outlines procedures for land valuation, compensation, and legal challenges. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1870, indicating acts passed during a legislative session.

It also covers the process for land acquisition by the railway company through eminent domain, including property views by commissioners and jury inquests.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Maysville and Big Sandy Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Maysville and Big Sandy Railroad Company to issue and sell bonds up to fifteen million dollars to finance its operations. It outlines procedures for securing these bonds through mortgages or deeds of trust, including foreclosure processes and the establishment of a sinking fund for bond redemption. monwealth of Kentucky .

ory caused by resignation,death, or otherwise, and make
allby-laws theymay deem necessaryforthe association.
