classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Town of Smithfield, in Henry County
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Town of Smithfield in Henry County, Kentucky. It establishes the initial trustees, outlines the process for electing future trustees, police judge, and town marshal, and grants the trustees powers to enact by-laws for town regulation and to levy taxes. Notably, it specifies that only 'free white persons' of a certain age and residency are qualified voters and eligible for office.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that 'all of the free white persons of the age of twenty-one years, citizensof said town , and having been citizensof said town for twelve months , shall be qualifiedvoters in the election of trustees,and enabled to hold office.' This is a clear racial qualification for voting and holding office, which is a hallmark of discriminatory practices during the Jim Crow era, even if it doesn't establish separate facilities or explicitly target Black individuals for punishment. The statute's focus on defining who is eligible to participate in local governance based on race makes it relevant to the Jim Crow archive. However, it is not a law that enforces segregation or differential treatment in public accommodations, education, or other core Jim Crow categories. It is primarily about the mechanics of local governance and voter eligibility. Therefore, while it contains a racial restriction, it doesn't fit the typical categories of Jim Crow laws like segregation or disenfranchisement in a broader sense. It is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow because its primary purpose is incorporation and governance, and the racial clause, while discriminatory, is a qualification for participation rather than a direct enforcement of segregation or suppression of rights in the typical Jim Crow sense. The confidence is high because the racial qualifier is explicit. Human review is not needed as the racial indicator is explicit and the statute's purpose is clear.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1870 outlines the process for appointing town assessors and assistant assessors. These officials are tasked with creating lists of inhabitants and property owners, valuing real estate and other taxable property as designated by the board. The statute details procedures for oath-taking, estimation of property value if the owner refuses to swear, and the inclusion of certain businesses and service providers. It also describes the process for examining these lists, making corrections for aggrieved parties, and the board's role in levying taxes. Additionally, it grants jurisdiction to a justice of the peace in the Auburn district over violations of town laws and ordinances, specifying fees for their services.

said heirs are very numerous ,and reside in not lessthan
six differentStates. Joseph Youce , the administrator,is
one of said heirs-at-law , and the only one residing in
Kentucky ; that said lots of land are not susceptibleof
divisionamong so great a number ; that allsaid heirs-at-
law have a common interest in the sale of said lots, and
that it would be to the interest of all of them for said lots
to be sold, and the proceeds to be distributed among
them ; but that said heirs-at-law being so very numerous ,
and the names and residences of some of them unknown ,
itiswholly impracticableto bring them all before the
court within a reasonable time,if,indeed,itcould be done
at all; therefore,

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Mount Gilead and Campbellsburg Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Mount Gilead and Campbellsburg Turnpike Road Company, outlining its powers, the election of its officers, and procedures for land acquisition and toll collection. It details the process for electing a president and three directors, their oath of office, and the treasurer's bond. The company is granted perpetual succession and the ability to hold, purchase, sell, and convey real and personal estate. It can sue and be sued, and erect toll gates. If land acquisition through agreement fails, the company can apply for a writ of ad quod damnum to assess damages, involving a jury of housekeepers summoned by the sheriff.
classification.reasoning: The statute clearly outlines the incorporation and operational procedures for a turnpike road company. It details corporate governance, property rights, and legal processes for land acquisition. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

in the case of the Commonwealth vs. O. Murphy , on in-
dictment for murder therein pending ,at its March term ,
1869 ; and whereas ,the said Samms did, in compliance
with his said recognizance,come from his home in Mis-
souri as a witness for the Commonwealth ,and remained

newed and conditioned for his appearance at the Sep-
tember term , 1869 , of the Gallatin circuit court ; and
whereas , the said Samms did come from his home in
Missouri again and appear in the Gallatin circuitcourt,
at its September term , 1869 , and in all things comply
with his said recognizances; and whereas , the judges of
said circuit courts made allowances to said William
Samms ,as a witness for the Commonwealth ,for mileage
and attendance in said case of the Commonwealth vs.
Murphy ,at the same rate as is now allowed by law to
witnesses residentin this State for similar services, and
the same were duly certifiedto the Auditor for auditing
and payment ,and thesame have been presentedfor pay-
ment ,and the Auditor has declined, on the advice of the
Attorney General ,to audit and draw on the Treasury for
the same ,being unprovided for by law ; now , therefore,
to do justicein the premises,Be itenactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That the Auditor of Public Accounts is hereby em-
powered and directedto audit the claims of the said Wil-
liam Samms , and draw his warrantson the Treasuryin
favor of the said William Samms ,for the sum of ninety-
one dollars and forty-four cents, as pay for his mileage
and attendance at the Carrollcircuit,at itsMarch term ,
1869, as witness in behalf of the Commonwealth in the
caseof the Commonwealth vs.O. Murphy ,on indictment
formurder,and for thefurthersum of ninety-two dollars
and eighty-four cents,as pay for his mileage and attend-
ance as a witnessfor the Commonwealth in the same case,
in the Gallatin circuitcourt,at itsSeptember term , 1869,
on the presentationof the orders of said circuitcourts,
duly certified,allowing to the saidWilliam Samms said

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act to allow the Citizens of Henry County to vote a Tax in aid of Turnpike Companies of said County.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning taxes for turnpike companies in Henry County. It specifies that the county court shall levy an annual tax on property within each district that has voted for such a tax. The tax is set at twenty cents per one hundred dollars of property value and a poll tax of fifty cents on each male over twenty-one years of age. The sheriff is responsible for collecting this tax along with state revenue, receiving a two percent commission, and remitting the funds monthly to a treasurer appointed by the county court judge. The treasurer is required to provide a receipt, keep and disburse funds, give bond, and take an oath of office.

It details the tax rates, collection responsibilities of the sheriff, and the role of a treasurer. classification.summary: This act incorporates the Rock Haven Manufacturing Company, granting it broad powers to engage in various agricultural, manufacturing, mercantile, and transportation businesses in Meade County, Kentucky. It details the company's authority to purchase and sell real estate, construct buildings and machinery, manufacture cotton and wool fabrics, process agricultural products, raise livestock, operate mills and factories, and build infrastructure like turnpikes and railroads.
classification.reasoning: The statute incorporates a manufacturing company and outlines its business operations. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1870.

The officers are to select papers with the largest bona fide daily circulation, and the advertisement costs will be taxed as other court costs, not exceeding the lowest cash rates charged by the papers. The advertisements must be in the name of the marshal and sheriff alone.

CHAPTER 1012 .
AN ACT forthe benefitof D. C. Donan ,of Hart County .
WHEREAS , There are several mill -dams across Little
Barren river; and whereas , all the mills on said stream
are on the north side, and inconvenient and difficultin
times of high water for the people on the south side of
the river to approach ; and whereas ,a mill on the south
of said stream would be a public benefit to all living in
a reasonable distanceof said stream and mill; therefore,
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyoftheCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That D. C. Donan ,who owns the southwest bank
of said riverat or near Crail'sFord , shall have the right
to build a mill -dam across said stream at the aforesaid
ford; and he shallalso have power to change the crossing
at the ford two hundred feet lower down said stream , and
open a road forthe benefitof the public from Crail'sFord ,
in Green county ,to Three Springs, in Hart county .
§ 2. This act shalltake effectfrom itspassage .
Approved March 21,1870.
CHAPTER 1013 .
AN ACT to amend an act to establisha Police Judge in the Town of
Carrollton, approved March 20, 1851.
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assemblyof the Commonwealth of
Kentucky :
§ 1. That in allcasesin the policecourtin the town of
Carrollton,in which persons are convicted, and a fineim-
posed ,and the same and the costsare not paid or replev-
ied,the defendant or defendants shall be made to perform
such labor as the board of trusteesof said town may by
ordinance direct, until the fine and costs are discharged at
the rate of one dollar per day ; and in all cases in which
the penalty isimprisonment in the county jail,the defend-
ant or defendants shallin likemanner be made toperform
such labor as said trusteesmay by ordinance direct; and
where the judgment isboth fineand imprisonment,shall
be required to labor as above directed.
§2 . That said trusteesmay by ordinance provide for
the control and safe-keeping of such persons,while they
are performing the labor required of them .
§ 3. This act to be in forcefrom the date of itspassage.
Approved March 21, 1870.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act to establish a Police Judge in the Town of Carrollton, approved March 20, 1851.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law establishing a Police Judge in Carrollton. It mandates that individuals convicted in the police court who cannot pay fines and costs, or who are sentenced to imprisonment, shall be made to perform labor as directed by the town's board of trustees until the fines and costs are discharged. The labor rate is set at one dollar per day. The trustees are also empowered to provide for the control and safe-keeping of these individuals.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An act to incorporate the city of Ludlow," approved March 10, 1860, and to confer additional powers upon the city council of said city.
classification.summary: This act grants the city of Ludlow the power to condemn land and materials for public improvements such as streets, alleys, commons, wharves, landings, market spaces, public squares, and ferries. It also specifically empowers the city to acquire ferry rights and franchises, and to establish, equip, control, and run ferries across the Ohio River. The mayor's court of Ludlow is given exclusive original jurisdiction over condemnation proceedings.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the powers of the city of Ludlow regarding public improvements and the operation of ferries. It details the legal processes for land condemnation and the jurisdiction of the mayor's court. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the legal procedures for the condemnation of private property for public use by a city in Kentucky. It details how to serve notice to proprietors, the appointment of attorneys for warned proprietors, the process of awarding a writ of ad quod damnum, the summoning and role of a jury to assess damages and value, and the court's judgment. It also covers appeals to higher courts and the payment of costs.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act details the process for land acquisition by the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company, including provisions for eminent domain when agreement cannot be reached with landowners. It outlines procedures for appointing commissioners to assess damages, reporting to the county clerk, issuing summons to landowners, and empaneling juries to determine compensation. Special provisions are made for non-resident landowners, femes covert, infants, and non compos mentis, including the appointment of attorneys to defend their interests. The act also specifies how damages are to be calculated, considering both the value of the land taken and incidental damages offset by advantages to remaining property. It sets a maximum width for condemned land for the roadway.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the legal procedures for land acquisition by a railroad company through eminent domain. It outlines the process for valuing land, compensating owners, and handling cases involving landowners who are non-residents, married women, minors, or of unsound mind. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1870, indicating it's an act passed during a legislative session. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on property rights and eminent domain procedures without any racial component.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company to acquire land, materials, and rights-of-way for the construction and repair of its roads and works. The statute addresses situations where property owners are absent, minors, or of unsound mind, and establishes a process for compensating owners for taken property and offsetting advantages to remaining land.

classification.title: An Act to Authorize Subscriptions to the Stock of the Guthrie City and Franklin Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act authorizes counties, cities, towns, and election districts in Kentucky to subscribe to the stock of the Guthrie City and Franklin Railroad Company. It details the process for issuing bonds to pay for these subscriptions, including denominations, interest rates, and payment locations. It also outlines the tax levy procedures to ensure the repayment of these bonds and their interest, specifying that the tax will be levied on all real and personal property subject to state taxation within the respective jurisdictions. A board of commissioners of the sinking fund is to be appointed for each county, city, or town issuing bonds.

It also outlines the responsibilities and powers of the board of directors, including setting meeting times, enacting by-laws, appointing officers, and managing company affairs. Furthermore, it describes the process for collecting stock subscriptions, including provisions for suing for unpaid installments or forfeiting stock, and establishes rules for record-keeping and evidence.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses corporate governance, specifically the voting rights of stockholders and the management of a company by its board of directors. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for is_jim_crow. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about corporate finance and management. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1870'. The racial indicator is 'none' as race is not mentioned.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Big Sandy River Bridge Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Big Sandy River Bridge Company, granting it powers to construct a bridge over the Big Sandy River. It outlines provisions for land acquisition, construction standards (not obstructing navigation), toll collection, penalties for arson, trespass, and toll evasion, and procedures for land condemnation. The rates of toll are to be no more than those authorized for the Covington and Cincinnati Bridge Company.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation and operational powers of a bridge company. It details the process of constructing a bridge, acquiring land, setting toll rates, and penalties for various offenses related to the bridge. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to amend the law in relation to Roads ," approved February 17, 1866, so far as the same applies to Warren County .
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning roads in Warren County. It allows the county judge to decide when a vote on the road law can be taken, overriding the previous requirement for the vote to occur at the first general election after the act's passage.

Neither of these acts mentions race, nor do they contain any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, they are classified as not Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: AN ACT Concerning Pond Creek, in Jefferson County
classification.summary: This act makes it unlawful for any person to cut and fell trees into or across Pond Creek in Jefferson County between specific points without immediately removing them. It also requires landowners along the creek between these points to remove drifts and drift piles annually before October 1st. Penalties are imposed for violations, including fines and recovery by warrant before a justice of the peace.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns environmental regulations related to a specific creek in Jefferson County, Kentucky. It addresses the obstruction of the creek by trees and drifts and sets penalties for violations. There is no mention of race, and the regulations apply universally to all individuals and landowners along the creek. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The specific entry for "Catlettsburg, act relating to the sale of ardentspirits in" is listed.

Jim Crow laws typically involved racial segregation or discrimination. This index entry does not contain any explicit or implicit racial indicators. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high because the text is clearly an index and lacks any discriminatory language. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an index to acts passed by the General Assembly. Human review is not needed as the classification is straightforward based on the provided text.

classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'Colored People' in its title and purpose. While it refers to a specific organization for Black individuals, it is an act of incorporation for a benevolent society, not a law that restricts rights or enforces segregation. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it appears to be an act passed during a legislative session, as indicated by the page numbering and the nature of the entry in the index.

classification.title: An Act to amend Chapter 10 of the General Statutes, concerning the assessment of taxes.
classification.summary: This act amends Chapter 10 of the General Statutes related to the assessment of taxes. It outlines procedures for the assessment of property and the collection of taxes, including provisions for appeals and penalties for non-compliance.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is an amendment to Chapter 10 of the General Statutes concerning the assessment of taxes. The text details procedures for property assessment and tax collection. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly.
